questions.question: What was the primary driver of the 22% increase in week-over-week traffic for The Wall Street Journal during Week 35?

options.label: A
options.text: The implementation of a new MMM-driven media plan.
options.rationale: While this improved media efficiency and orders, it was not cited as the primary driver for the overall surge in unique visitors.

options.label: B
options.text: Live coverage of U.S. military strikes in Iran.
options.rationale: The report explicitly identifies live coverage of the conflict as the main factor driving direct and search traffic.

options.label: C
options.text: Viral social media sharing of the Citrini Research report.
options.rationale: The AI report caused market panic and high conversions for one article, but the broader traffic surge was attributed to the geopolitical news cycle.

options.label: D
options.text: A record-breaking performance of the Win-back program.
options.rationale: The Win-back program focuses on re-acquiring former subscribers rather than driving the primary surge in new unique visitors.

questions.question: By what percentage did WSJ International (INTL) outperform its traffic forecast for the week?

options.label: A
options.rationale: This figure represents the total week-over-week increase for all WSJ reach, not the INTL forecast variance.

options.label: B
options.rationale: This was the actual week-over-week growth percentage for INTL, rather than the comparison to its forecast.

options.label: C
options.rationale: The performance summary notes that the 2.2 million unique visitors exceeded the projected forecast by this specific margin.

options.label: D
options.rationale: This percentage refers to the increase in lower-funnel media spend in the US market.

questions.question: Following the shift to an MMM-driven Full Funnel media plan, what was the resulting Cost Per Order (CPO) for paid media in the US?

options.label: A
options.rationale: This lower figure was the record-breaking CPO specifically achieved by the Win-back program using Meta CAPI.

options.label: B
options.rationale: The shift in media strategy allowed the US market to scale spending while maintaining this highly efficient acquisition cost.

options.label: C
options.rationale: This value corresponds to the dollar amount of week-over-week revenue lift rather than a cost metric.

options.label: D
options.rationale: This number represents the percentage of the order forecast hit by the media plan, not a dollar cost.

questions.question: Which platform saw the highest week-over-week growth in order volume during Week 35?

options.label: A
options.text: WSJ US Web
options.rationale: Web orders saw a significant 29% increase, but this was outperformed by growth on another digital platform.

options.label: B
options.text: WSJ International
options.rationale: While traffic for International was high, the specific growth in order volume for the app was higher.

options.label: C
options.text: WSJ App
options.rationale: Orders on the mobile application surged by 52%, representing the strongest growth among the different digital platforms.

options.label: D
options.text: WSJ Global Digital
options.rationale: The 35% growth for Global Digital serves as a total average that was exceeded by specific individual platforms.

questions.question: According to the WSJ report on migration, what was the net population loss for the U.S. in 2025?

options.label: A
options.text: 361,189 people
options.rationale: This figure actually refers to the number of unique visitors who read the top-converting article.

options.label: B
options.text: 150,000 people
options.rationale: The article highlights this record estimate for net negative migration as Americans moved abroad in record numbers.

options.label: C
options.text: 1,003 people
options.rationale: This number represents the total weekly orders for the Win-back program, not population data.

options.label: D
options.text: 822,000 people
options.rationale: This is a misinterpretation of the Dow Jones industrial average drop magnitude.

questions.question: The Citrini Research report caused market anxiety by predicting a 'global intelligence crisis' by which year?

options.label: A
options.rationale: This was the year cited for the migration shift, rather than the predicted financial crisis.

options.label: B
options.rationale: This is the current year of the performance report, not the future date envisioned in the doomsday scenario.

options.label: C
options.rationale: The hypothetical report envisioned massive AI-driven spending cuts and unemployment reaching a critical point by this date.

options.label: D
options.rationale: While a common milestone for future predictions, the specific report mentioned a slightly earlier timeframe.

questions.question: Why did the market sell-off specifically target private credit lenders and software firms following the viral AI report?

options.label: A
options.text: Because they are the primary developers of the disruptive AI technologies.
options.rationale: While they use AI, the anxiety was focused on their reliance on human productivity rather than their development of the tech.

options.label: B
options.text: Because these sectors rely heavily on white-collar productivity.
options.rationale: The report suggested AI would lead to widespread white-collar unemployment, threatening the business models of these specific industries.

options.label: C
options.text: Because they were the primary investors in Iranian energy infrastructure.
options.rationale: The sell-off was linked to internal market anxieties regarding AI, not the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East.

options.label: D
options.text: Because of an 822-point rise in interest rates by the Federal Reserve.
options.rationale: The 822-point figure refers to a drop in the Dow Jones index, not an interest rate hike.

questions.question: What tactical opportunity led the U.S. and Israel to launch airstrikes against Iran during the day?

options.label: A
options.text: The discovery of a new nuclear enrichment facility.
options.rationale: While nuclear issues are often central to Iran relations, the text specifies the strikes targeted leadership meetings.

options.label: B
options.text: The occurrence of simultaneous meetings of senior political and military leaders.
options.rationale: Intelligence identified a rare moment where multiple high-level officials, including the Supreme Leader, were gathered together.

options.label: C
options.text: A failure in Iran's national cyber-defense grid.
options.rationale: Cyberattacks were part of the campaign, but they were a tool used alongside the strikes rather than the reason for the timing.

options.label: D
options.text: A formal request for intervention by the Iranian armed forces.
options.rationale: The strikes were paired with messages *urging* the forces to rebel, indicating that cooperation was not the initial trigger.

questions.question: In addition to the physical airstrikes, what other method was used by the U.S. and Israel to encourage regime change in Iran?

options.label: A
options.text: A full naval blockade of the Persian Gulf.
options.rationale: The text does not mention naval maneuvers or blockades as part of the specific tactical execution.

options.label: B
options.text: Broad cyberattacks urging citizens and the military to rebel.
options.rationale: The military campaign was supplemented by digital efforts to destabilize the government from within.

options.label: C
options.text: Economic sanctions targeting the export of software to Iran.
options.rationale: While sanctions are a standard policy tool, the text focuses on the active air and cyber campaign during the strikes.

options.label: D
options.text: The deployment of ground troops to secure major urban centers.
options.rationale: The report limits the military description to air campaigns and cyber-warfare.

questions.question: How did WSJ's retention performance compare to its budget during this news-heavy week?

options.label: A
options.text: Stops were 16% higher than budget due to the volatile news cycle.
options.rationale: Actually, the high news interest usually correlates with better retention, resulting in stops being lower than expected.

options.label: B
options.text: Stops were exactly on budget despite the traffic surge.
options.rationale: The report indicates a favorable variance where actual stops were significantly below the budgeted amounts.

options.label: C
options.text: Stops were 16% below budget, indicating strong retention.
options.rationale: The 'favorable stop rates' mention that overall global stops were significantly lower than the anticipated budget.

options.label: D
options.text: Trialist stops were higher than in-life stops relative to budget.
options.rationale: Trialist stops were actually 19% below budget, performing even better against projections than in-life stops at 13% below.
